How Virtual Commissioning
Reduces Project Risk.

Published April 2025 · 5 min read

Our VC ServiceGet In Touch

For automotive manufacturers and their automation partners, the commissioning phase of a project has always been one of the highest-risk periods. Equipment is powered up for the first time, robots move through programs that have never been tested in the real world, and the pressure to meet production start dates is intense.

What is Virtual Commissioning?

Virtual commissioning is the process of creating a digital twin of an automation system — including robots, PLCs, conveyors and end-of-line equipment — and using that model to test and validate control software before a single engineer steps on site.

Using tools such as Siemens Process Simulate, engineers can connect emulated PLCs and real robot controllers to a 3D simulation model of the complete system. Programs are tested, sequences are validated, and faults are discovered in a safe, virtual environment.

The Benefits in Practice

On a recent automotive BIW project involving over 40 robots, Duke Control Systems used virtual commissioning to reduce on-site commissioning time by approximately 30%. Sequence conflicts that would previously have required days to identify and resolve on site were found and corrected in simulation weeks before the project reached the factory floor.

The benefits are particularly significant on complex multi-robot lines where interlocking between cells, zone control and production line synchronisation all need to work together correctly before production can start.

Is Virtual Commissioning Right for Your Project?

Virtual commissioning delivers the greatest return on investment on larger, more complex projects — particularly those with multiple robots, complex PLC interlocking, or tight production start dates where on-site delays would be costly.

If you're planning an automation project and would like to understand how virtual commissioning could reduce your risk and commissioning time, get in touch for a free consultation.
